html:
MEMSCAP (MEMS.PA)

MEMSCAP is no longer the story in the old drafts. The public record does not support the cleaner but likely wrong “Safran inertial sensor LTA” narrative. The real thesis is smaller, more prosaic, and better: MEMSCAP is a €40.8 million French micro-cap that has turned itself from a capital-heavy MEMS foundry into a focused, profitable supplier of ultra-stable pressure sensors for aerospace and medical applications, plus fabless optical MEMS chips for telecom, data center, and HPC infrastructure.

At the current price of about €5.36, the stock is not statistically cheap on trailing numbers: 2025 revenue was only €12.0 million, adjusted EBITDA was €1.58 million, and net income was €0.89 million. That makes the trailing P/E look expensive. The variant perception is that trailing earnings are the wrong denominator because 2025 included a severe medical customer trough, while the growth vectors that matter are only starting to show up: aerospace “Engine Control,” new “Fluidic Systems” applications with Parker Hannifin, and a sharp recovery in optical communications tied to data center and HPC investment.

The business transition matters. MEMSCAP completed its “Fablite” shift in 2022 by selling its North Carolina fab while retaining supply for optical MEMS chips. That left a much leaner company with 54 employees, no bank debt, €6.0 million of available cash, and a more product-led model. This is not a speculative revenue story burning cash while waiting for qualification. It is already profitable, R&D is expensed, and the company generated positive operating cash flow in 2025.

The most important 2025 data point is hidden by the headline revenue decline. Full-year revenue fell 5.3% because medical revenue collapsed 53.2% after two long-standing customers reduced orders. But aerospace grew 7.5% to €8.8 million, becoming 73.5% of revenue, and optical communications grew 31.4%. In Q4 2025, revenue grew 17%, with aerospace up 25%, driven by Engine Control. In Q1 2026, revenue grew 6.2% reported and 12.6% at constant exchange rates, with optical communications nearly doubling to €734k, explicitly helped by data center and HPC transmission demand.

The Parker Hannifin development agreement is the best forward signal. MEMSCAP will customize pressure sensors and modules for Parker fluidic aerospace systems. Management says MEMSCAP is already a market leader in air data computers, navigation systems, and cabin pressure control, has deployed solutions for engine control, and has completed technical readiness review in that segment. Parker extends the addressable market into airframe fluidic systems. The company says aerospace pressure sensing is a market of several hundred million dollars annually. MEMSCAP does not need a large share of that market for the stock to work. On this revenue base, €3 million to €5 million of incremental annual revenue is transformative.

That is the torque. The fixed cost base is now small, not bloated. 2025 operating expenses were just under €4.0 million. Gross margin was 41.4% despite the medical collapse. If revenue moves from €12 million in 2025 to €17 million to €18 million in 2027, with gross margin in the low-to-mid 40s and opex growing modestly, EBITDA can move toward €3.5 million to €4.2 million and EPS toward €0.30 to €0.35. That would put today’s stock around the high teens on 2027 earnings and roughly 8x to 10x forward ex-cash EBITDA, for a qualified aerospace sensor supplier with positive cash flow and no bank debt.

The market still treats this like an obscure French micro-cap, not a strategic aerospace component supplier. Retail discussion is thin, mostly Boursorama message-board chatter about the AGM, technical levels, and GreenSome’s target raise to €7.06. Visible institutional ownership is minimal. The stock has had violent moves, but it is not a crowded retail phenomenon. The setup is closer to orphaned small-cap discovery than hype.

The risks are real. Q1 2026 aerospace revenue was down reported because of FX, and Q1 gross margin fell to 38.8%. The medical recovery is not proven. Optical communications is cyclical and exposed to Asian telecom/data infrastructure ordering. Parker is a development agreement, not yet a disclosed production contract. Liquidity is poor, and a missed Q2 or H1 could take the stock back toward the old micro-cap range quickly.

But the balance sheet gives time. Cash is about €0.78 per share, there is no bank financial debt excluding leases, shareholders’ equity is €19.0 million, and the board proposed a first dividend of €0.066 per share. This is not downside-free, but the company is not dependent on capital markets to bridge the transition.

The cleanest thesis is: MEMSCAP is a newly profitable, underfollowed aerospace pressure-sensor company whose 2025 trough earnings mask a mix shift into higher-value aerospace applications and a suddenly relevant optical MEMS/HPC tailwind. The market is paying up on trailing numbers but not paying much for the probability that 2027 earnings power is materially above the last reported year.

Bear case: €3.50, about 35% downside. Medical stays weak, Parker remains development-only, optical fades, and revenue stays near €12 million to €13 million with EBITDA stuck around €1.5 million.

Base case: €7.50, about 40% upside. Revenue reaches €17 million to €18 million by 2027, EBITDA approaches €4 million, and the stock trades near 13x ex-cash EBITDA for a small but profitable aerospace MEMS supplier.

Bull case: €11.50, about 115% upside. Engine Control and Fluidic Systems convert into serial production, optical benefits from data center/HPC spending, revenue passes €22 million, EBITDA reaches €5.5 million to €6.5 million, and MEMSCAP is re-rated as a scarce strategic aerospace sensor asset rather than a forgotten micro-cap.
